title: Against-Medical-Advice (AMA) Discharge: Honoring a Capable Patient's Informed Refusal
subtitle: A 52-year-old man admitted with leg cellulitis and early sepsis is improving on IV antibiotics but wants to leave AMA after one day for work and childcare reasons. He is alert and decisional. The nurse must protect his rights, avoid coercion, and reduce harm.

patient.name: Marcus Webb
patient.admitDate: Admitted ~18 hours ago with lower-leg cellulitis and early sepsis; started on IV antibiotics and fluids. Clinically improving but IV course incomplete. Alert, oriented, decisional. States he must leave today for work and childcare; verbalizes he understands he is leaving against advice.

vitals.temp: Calm; adamant about leaving for work/childcare

labs.v: Understands risks/benefits/alternatives; reasons; states a choice.
Risks explained; provider notified; form offered.

nursesNote: Marcus Webb, 52, ~18 h into treatment for leg cellulitis with early sepsis; improving on IV antibiotics but IV course incomplete. Alert, oriented, decisional; wants to leave AMA today for work/childcare. No coercion used. Risks/benefits explained, provider notified, barriers explored, harm-reduction (oral antibiotics, follow-up, return precautions) arranged.
providerOrders: Continue IV antibiotics and fluids for cellulitis/early sepsis
If patient elects to leave: assess capacity and document informed refusal
Provide oral antibiotic prescription, follow-up, and return precautions
Notify provider of any decision to leave against medical advice
Do not coerce or physically detain a capable, decisional patient

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: A capable patient is requesting an AMA discharge — what applies?

opts.text: Assess and confirm his decisional capacity
opts.rat: Capacity grounds the right to refuse.

opts.text: A capable adult may leave even against medical advice
Notify the provider promptly of his intent to leave
Clearly inform him of the risks of leaving and benefits of staying
You may physically restrain him to prevent leaving
Explore and try to address his reasons (work, childcare, cost)

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is true about capacity and AMA?

opts.text: Capacity = understands info, appreciates consequences, reasons, states a choice
Leaving AMA does not forfeit discharge teaching, prescriptions, or follow-up
Refusing to sign the AMA form does not void his right to leave
opts.rat: The form is not consent to detain.

opts.text: Coercion, threats, or shaming are inappropriate
Because he is leaving AMA, no return precautions are needed
An incomplete IV course risks worsening cellulitis, abscess, or sepsis
Confirm decisional capacity
Notify the provider of his intent to leave
Honestly explain risks of leaving and benefits of staying
Explore barriers and offer harm-reduction (oral antibiotics, follow-up)
Document objectively and offer the AMA form, teaching, and resources
Confirm capacity before honoring the refusal
Provide honest risk/benefit information
Offer prescriptions, follow-up, and return precautions
Notify the provider and document objectively
Address barriers (transport, childcare, cost) where possible
Let him leave once informed refusal is established
Physically block the door or restrain him
Threaten that insurance won't pay if he leaves
Withhold prescriptions and teaching as leverage
Shame him for being 'noncompliant'

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence the AMA process at the bedside
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm capacity and that the refusal is informed
Notify the provider of the intent to leave
Give honest risk/benefit counseling and address barriers
Arrange harm-reduction: prescriptions, follow-up, return precautions, teaching
Document objectively, offer the AMA form, and ensure a safe exit
The nurse confirmed capacity and honored the informed refusal
Risks and benefits were explained honestly and questions answered
Oral antibiotics, follow-up, and return precautions were provided
The provider was notified and the event documented objectively
Barriers were explored and addressed where possible
He was told he could not leave without signing the AMA form
opts.rat: Signing is not required to leave.

opts.text: Staff threatened him with the cost of leaving
Prescriptions were withheld to discourage departure
Security physically detained the capable, decisional patient
